Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Apr-01-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Amy Eskin, Esq. (415.421.7100; aeskin@schneiderwallace.com) of Schneider Wallace Cottrell Konechy LLP for Plaintiffs.
Richard V. De Gruccio, Esq. (916.288.2248; Richard@chs.law) of Christensen Hsu Sipes, LLP for Defendant, Exclusive Fresh, Inc.
Fred Wiesner, Esq. (916.503.2777; Fred.wiesner@farmersinsurance.com) of Stratman & williams-Abrego for Defendant, Sam's Chowder House LP.
Jianlin Song, Esq. (415.625.9259; jianlin.song@wilsonelser.com) of Wilson Elser Moskowitz, Edelman& Dicker LLP for Defendant, Pacific Northwest Shellfish Company LTD.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SAM'S CHOWDER HOUSE, LP's Motion For Order Determining Good Faith Settlement is granted. Once, as here, a settlor makes a preliminary showing, a non-settling party has the burden of demonstrating that a settlement is "so far 'out of the ballpark'" as to be inconsistent with the law's equitable objectives. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499-500.) Experienced counsel for Sam's and plaintiffs negotiated a $300,000 settlement. No evidence suggests collusion, fraud or tortious conduct. (Id.) A co-defendant argues that plaintiffs once demanded $34 million in damages. At the same time, all co-defendants insist they have no liability, and Sam's says the $300,000 is a cost-of-defense settlement. It is also argued that Sam's should pay more due to its insurance policy limits, but no authority is cited for that notion.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Discussed. Plaintiff reports it withdraws its request to contest the tentative ruling. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SAM'S CHOWDER HOUSE, LP'S MOTION TO STRIKE PRAYER FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES.

Defendant Sam's Chowder House, LP's motion to strike prayer for punitive damages is granted.

In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) (see word minutes for full details)
